question_title: Will Turkey's Court of Cassation rule on CHP's appeal of the "absolute nullity" congress ruling before July 12, 2026?
summary: As of mid-to-late June 2026, CHP's appeal file had not even reached the Yarg\u0131tay registry (confirmed by court officials June 14), with the reasoned BAM decision, service, registration, rapporteur report, and agenda placement all still pending. The closest precedent (Cumhuriyet Vakf\u0131 nullity case, same chamber) took ~73 days from chamber receipt to ruling. Commentators and legal sources broadly expect a September decision after the July 20-Sept 1 judicial recess; even the optimistic pre-recess scenario was deemed unlikely by sympathetic press. The question's window (closing July 11) is tighter still than the recess deadline. YES would require both file arrival and unusually fast action (e.g., a quick inadmissibility dismissal or interim-measure decision) within days. I estimate ~4%, lifting slightly above the pure factor-sum to account for the news-index coverage gap after ~June 28 and tail risk of expedited political action.

factors.description: As of June 14 2026, Yargıtay officials confirmed the BAM case file had not even physically reached the Court of Cassation registry; no rapporteur report, agenda placement, or hearing date
Precedent (analogous Cumhuriyet Vakfı nullity case, same chamber): ~73 days from file reaching chamber to ruling; window here is ~2 weeks and the file hadn't reached the chamber yet
Commentators and AKP/legal sources broadly expect a September ruling (after the July 20-Sept 1 recess); even the optimistic pre-recess scenario deemed unlikely by sympathetic press
Question window (July 11) is even tighter than the recess deadline (July 20), making a qualifying ruling harder still
Politically charged high-profile case could prompt expedited action, or a quick inadmissibility dismissal/interim-measure decision once the file arrives

reasoning:
**Forecast: 4% (NO)**

Turkey's Court of Cassation is very unlikely to rule on CHP's appeal between June 27 and July 11, 2026. The decisive fact: as of mid-June, the case file had not even physically reached the Yargıtay — and the analogous Cumhuriyet Vakfı precedent took ~73 days from chamber receipt to ruling. Turkish legal commentators and AKP sources broadly expect a September decision after the July 20–Sept 1 judicial recess, with even the optimistic pre-recess scenario deemed unlikely. The question's July 11 cutoff is tighter still. I set it slightly above the raw factor-sum to allow for the news-coverage gap after ~June 28 and a small chance of expedited political action.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between June 27, 2026 and July 11, 2026 (inclusive), Turkey's Court of Cassation (Yargıtay) issues a ruling (a decision either upholding/approving, reversing, partially reversing, or dismissing) on CHP's appeal of the May 21, 2026 Ankara Regional Court of Appeals 36th Civil Chamber "absolute nullity" (mutlak butlan) decision concerning CHP's 38th Ordinary Congress, as reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). It resolves NO if no such Yargıtay ruling is reported within that window.
fine_print: A "ruling" means a decision by the relevant Yargıtay civil chamber that disposes of the appeal: an onama (approval), bozma (reversal), partial reversal, or a decision dismissing/rejecting the appeal (including a dismissal on inadmissibility grounds, such as a finding that temyiz is unavailable for mutlak butlan rulings) — all of these count as qualifying rulings. A decision on the appeal of the interim measure (ihtiyati tedbir) also counts. Purely procedural or administrative steps — such as the file being registered at the Yargıtay, assignment to a chamber, a rapporteur (tetkik hakimi) report being prepared, or a hearing date being scheduled — do NOT count. The decision counts when it is announced/issued, regardless of when a written reasoned opinion is published. If the date of the ruling is disputed, the date the chamber rendered the decision (as reported) governs. If no qualifying ruling is reported by July 11, 2026 (23:59 Turkey Time, UTC+3), the question resolves NO.

factors.description: As of June 14 2026, Yargıtay officials confirmed the BAM case file had not even physically reached the Court of Cassation registry; no rapporteur report, no agenda placement, no hearing date set
Precedent (analogous Cumhuriyet Vakfı nullity case, same chamber): ~73 days from file reaching chamber to ruling. Window here is only ~2 weeks and the file hadn't reached the chamber yet
Commentators and AKP/legal sources broadly expect a September ruling (after the July 20-Sept 1 recess); even the optimistic pre-recess scenario deemed unlikely by sympathetic press
Question window (July 11) is even tighter than the recess deadline (July 20), making a qualifying ruling harder still
Politically charged high-profile case could prompt expedited action, or a quick inadmissibility dismissal once file arrives

input.anchor: Base rate for a high court issuing a ruling on a complex, high-profile civil appeal within a specific ~2-week window, when the file has not yet reached the court: very low, ~5%
input.assessment: This is a forward-looking predictive question with a very short window (June 27-July 11). Status quo strongly favors NO: as of mid-June the file had not reached the Yargıtay, and multiple procedural steps (reasoned BAM decision, service, registration, rapporteur report, agenda) remain. The closest precedent shows ~73 days from chamber receipt to ruling. Commentary consensus points to September. The only YES paths are an unusually expedited ruling or a fast inadmissibility dismissal, both requiring the file to first arrive and then be acted on within days — very unlikely. I estimate ~4%.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether a sudden political directive could force an expedited ruling; whether news coverage gap hides any early-July action (no positive evidence found)
input.tool_audit: mcp__research__research returned a thorough, well-sourced answer with concrete dates and a precedent benchmark — highly useful. No tool failures.
input.process_reflection: The research sub-agent worked well for this fast-moving Turkish political/legal question, surfacing Turkish-language sources I couldn't easily parse. The main caveat noted (news index coverage tops out ~June 28) is a genuine limitation but doesn't change the conclusion given the procedural bottleneck. No friction.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All five factors are grounded in the research trace:

- Factor 1 (file not yet at Yargıtay registry as of June 14): directly confirmed — "Yargıtay officials told Sözcü (June 14) the BAM case file had not physically reached the Court of Cassation." Data point present (20260614).
- Factor 2 (~73-day precedent, same chamber, Cumhuriyet Vakfı): confirmed by trace narrative and data point (value 73, days from file reaching chamber to ruling).
- Factor 3 (commentary expects September; pre-recess deemed unlikely): confirmed — AKP-kulis sources point to September; Selvi (June 26) "Ama pek ihtimal verilmiyor."
- Factor 4 (July 11 window tighter than July 20 recess): logically sound, correct direction.
- Factor 5 (+0.6, expedited/inadmissibility upside): correct positive sign, modest weight.

No wrong-direction factors, no fabricated core claims, no forecast-changing double-counting. Resolution criteria are provided and the agent engages with the specific terms well (inadmissibility dismissal, interim-measure/ihtiyati tedbir appeal, "announced regardless of written opinion" all addressed in factor 5 and the counterargument). No pre-publication-event issue: the question is forward-looking and nothing has resolved it.

Cross-question consistency: an earlier (June 17) worldview entry stated the appeal "is expected to be decided before July 20," but that reflects the Özel camp's hoped-for deadline, not a prediction; the agent's fresher June 29 research explicitly reports even sympathetic press views a pre-recess ruling as unlikely. The later, more granular research appropriately supersedes the earlier framing — no genuine contradiction.

Tool audit: agent claims "no tool failures." The trace shows an initial bad call ("No such tool available: research") before the correct mcp__research__research call, but the ground-truth metrics (2 calls, 0 errors) do not count that attempt, so the claim is consistent with the provided metrics. Trivial.

Independent probability: the evidence is strongly and one-directionally NO — file not yet at the court ~2 weeks before the window, multiple procedural steps remaining, 73-day post-arrival precedent, September consensus, and a ~2-week window. YES requires both file arrival AND fast action within days. My estimate is ~3–6%, consistent with the agent's 4%. The lift from the ~0.4% factor-sum to 4% to cover the news-index coverage gap (tops out ~June 28) and tail risk of expedited action is well-reasoned, not unjustified hedging toward a round number.

Counterargument is substantive — it constructs a coherent YES story (political expedition, fast inadmissibility dismissal, qualifying interim-measure decision, post-June-28 coverage blind spot) rather than restating the conclusion. Confidence of 0.85 is reasonable given decisive sourcing, tempered appropriately by the acknowledged index gap. No errors warranting warn or fail.
